coredata.dc:description: Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are promising receptors for a wide range of targeted molecules. In recent years, the construction of low molecular weight materials became possible based on the idea of molecular imprinting. The recent applications of MIPs are discussed with a brief computational chemistry to illustrate the design of MIPs. The different routes for the covalent and non-covalent imprinting in MIPs are highlighted in this review. The incorporation of optical, thermo-responsive, magnetic, and photocatalytic materials facilitated the development of multifunctional hybrid molecularly imprinted materials. MIPs have enormous beneficial aspects like high selectivity and specificity toward the targeted molecules. They are easy to synthesize, cost-effective, and reliable at different conditions like pH, temperature, and pressure. Additionally, the changeable properties of MIPs could be achieved by the different combinations of the templates, functional monomers, and crosslinkers. Based on these features, this review summarizes the fundamental concepts, recent advances, and applications of MIPs in multiple fields like environmental restoration, wastewater treatments, food analysis, chemical/biological sensing, targeted drug delivery, proteins recognition/purification, and hormone detoxification. The stated applications can be performed using MIPs as sorbents, stationary phases, and catalysts. Furthermore, the review features prospects and suggestions for substantial benefits from MIPs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are promising receptors for a wide range of targeted molecules. In recent years, the construction of low molecular weight materials became possible based on the idea of molecular imprinting. The recent applications of MIPs are discussed with a brief computational chemistry to illustrate the design of MIPs. The different routes for the covalent and non-covalent imprinting in MIPs are highlighted in this review. The incorporation of optical, thermo-responsive, magnetic, and photocatalytic materials facilitated the development of multifunctional hybrid molecularly imprinted materials. MIPs have enormous beneficial aspects like high selectivity and specificity toward the targeted molecules. They are easy to synthesize, cost-effective, and reliable at different conditions like pH, temperature, and pressure. Additionally, the changeable properties of MIPs could be achieved by the different combinations of the templates, functional monomers, and crosslinkers. Based on these features, this review summarizes the fundamental concepts, recent advances, and applications of MIPs in multiple fields like environmental restoration, wastewater treatments, food analysis, chemical/biological sensing, targeted drug delivery, proteins recognition/purification, and hormone detoxification. The stated applications can be performed using MIPs as sorbents, stationary phases, and catalysts. Furthermore, the review features prospects and suggestions for substantial benefits from MIPs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Probing the various analytes using contemporary polymeric materials is the center of fundamental and applied research interests. This research focus has proven to be fruitful in many aspects like sensing the contaminants in the environment, analysis of food items, and functionalization of electrochemical devices [1–3]. A vital area of sensing is molecular recognition, which could be explained based on the biomimicking of naturally occurring molecules. In the naturally occurring molecular recognition, antibodies (guest) and antigen (host) build non-covalent interactions such as hydrogen bonding, van der Waal force, resonant interaction, and metal coordination [4]. This interaction is important in biomedicine and biotechnology. The indispensable feature of molecular recognition is its specificity toward the targeted molecule. The antibodies allow only selective antigens to interact with them [5,6]. Medical and biological applications like biosensors [7–9] are based on this principle. Although naturally occurring proteinaceous antibodies have proven to be effective, however, their applications are limited [10]. These limitations include a short life span, low stability under harsh conditions, high cost, and large-scale production difficulty. To avoid such challenges, an alternative approach was introduced called molecular imprinting, which gives rise to molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) [11–13].

MIPs presented enormous benefits by minimizing the anomalies associated with natural molecular recognition [14]. They are referred to as the “chemical mimics” or “plastic/artificial antibodies”. Moreover, MIPs function using the lock and key model [15]. The components of MIPs consist of functional monomers, crosslinkers, template molecules, porogenic solvents, and initiators [16,17]. MIPs are synthesized following the copolymerization method [18]. A complex between the functional monomers and the targeted template is formed in the presence of a crosslinker. Once formed, the template is removed from the complex leaving behind a cavity that is complementary in shape and structure to the template. Hence, the synthesized MIPs could be used for the recognition of a specific template (analyte) [19–21].

MIPs have enormous beneficial aspects like high selectivity and specificity toward the targeted molecules. They are easy to synthesize, cost-effective, and reliable at different conditions like pH, temperature, and pressure [22]. Additionally, the changeable properties of MIPs could be achieved by the different combinations of the templates, functional monomers, and crosslinkers. Based on these features, MIPs are used in multiple fields like environmental restoration, wastewater treatments, food analysis, chemical/biological sensing, targeted drug delivery, proteins recognition/purification, and hormone detoxification [23–28]. The stated applications can be performed using MIPs as sorbents [29], stationary phases [30], and catalysts [31].

One of the most studied applications of MIPs is sensors fabrication. The basic principle of sensors is the conversion of the chemical into an electrical signal [32]. Sensors are designed to contain a receptor and a transducer. The receptors are responsible for the analyte detection and the transducer produces a measurable signal [33]. Abundant literature is available on the synthesis and different applications of polymers including MIPs [34–38]. This review focuses on the history, synthesis, and applications of MPIs in different fields. The strategy of MIPs synthesis is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

Our interest and others in the polymer chemistry field and MIPs stemmed from the diverse directions in the synthesis of small to macromolecules including their nanocomposites [17,20,21,25,27,28]. In addition, their applications in different aspects have added high value to the interest in their basic and applied research. Take-home messages can be taken from the long-term research in this field in adding more novel directions to study the interesting chemistry of MIPs parallel with manufacturing devices. Novel directions in this research are the ability to design and develop MIPs decorated with groups oriented in specific directions to immobilize specific analytes. Certainly, with the growing focus in this research on the enhancement MIPs stabilization and notable merge with calculations studies, nanocomposites, and manufacturing in collaboration with the industry we believe that more novel hybrid systems will be synthesized and others from different fields will be attracted to motivate additional innovative applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of MIPs from a historical context
ce:section.ce:para: A breakthrough in molecular imprinting synthesis started in the 1970 s when the research focus was diverted from silica-based materials to synthetic polymers [1,39–47]. Wulff, Mosbach, and Shea pioneered molecular imprinting development [48–61]. Initially, Wulff investigated the synthesis of polymers having active sites like those of enzymes [50]. In 1972, they compiled the findings regarding molecular imprinting with a focus on radical polymerization and polycondensation of polyurethanes using covalent and non-covalent interactions. The findings by Wulff were focused on the covalent interactions between the host and targeted molecule [50]. Shea investigated molecular imprinting with a focus on the bis-ketals [51]. Shea studied the rebinding of small molecules with the imprinted receptor sites using infrared spectroscopy and solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance. Mosbach, whose areas of research were cell immobilization, enzyme engineering, and affinity chromatography, contributed to molecular imprinting developments [52–55].

Research has progressed on molecular imprinting using polymer gels and enzymes [56]. Initially, the findings by Mosbach were entitled ‘host–guest polymerization’ although the term ‘imprint’ was also used frequently. It was until 1992 that the term ‘molecularly imprinted polymers’ abbreviated as ‘MIPs’ came into existence [57]. Looking at the history of MIPs over the last 20 years, enormous research articles, review articles, and books were published [58–61]. Literature on MIPs extends their validity in various applications like catalysis [62], sensors [63,64], and chromatography [65]. A step forward has also been made by the outbreak of the computational modeling studies paving the way for better rational designs and outputs of MIPs [66].

To synthesize MIPs, a brief background of the experimental conditions and the fundamental knowledge is essential. Basic knowledge must be collected about the equilibrium of the components needed in molecular imprinting, polymer chemistry, thermodynamics, computational background, and morphological assay [67,68].

One of the most challenges in developing MIPs is regulating the operating variables like components and optimum ratios. A closer look at the literature can provide abundant knowledge related to the building blocks of MIPs including the templates, initiators, monomers, crosslinkers, and solvents [69,70].

While assembling a template for a specific analyte, it is used as a template during the polymerization of functional monomers. Later, the template is removed which leaves a void inside MIPs structures [71]. This void is compatible with the template used earlier. The sensitivity of MIPs cavity toward the template improves the chance of binding to the analyte. The choice of the template is critical while developing MIPs [72]. The first condition for choosing a template is its chemical inertness, which must contain functional groups that do not interfere with the polymerization. The presence of polymerizable functional groups is also necessary for choosing a template [73–75]. Other factors that must be considered are the high number of binding sites, cost, shape, and template-monomer unbending nature. The common templates include CH3Hg+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Cu2+ [76], explosives [77], pesticides [78], amino acids [79], pharmaceuticals [80], peptides [81], endocrine-disrupting chemicals [82], and sugars [83]. Polymerization usually starts under vigorous conditions like high temperature or UV light irradiation. This requires high thermal and chemical stability of the template to tolerate such harsh conditions. Fig. 2 displays some common MIPs templates.

Another key in building the template is the functional monomers [84]. Functional monomers compactly surround the template and bind via non-covalent interaction [85]. The compatible functionality of the monomers-template pair should be as such that if one of them is H-donor and the other is H-acceptor [86]. More than one functional monomer could be used giving rise to cocktail polymerization. However, the main consideration while using two or more functional monomers is the ratio that drives a smooth copolymerization. The ratio of functional monomers to the template is another factor to optimize. The functional monomers are usually added in excess compared with the template, e.g., 1:4 for template: functional monomer [87–92]. Fig. 3 displays some functional monomers.

The compact and rigid structure of MIPs depends on the choice of the crosslinker (Fig. 4 ). The crosslinkers act as the glue between the templates and the functional monomers [93]. The structure of MIPs dictates the formation of a macroporous, microgel powder, or gel [94,95]. The stability of the binding sites of MIPs is also influenced by the crosslinkers. The ratio of crosslinkers also has a strong influence on polymerization. Normally, an excess amount of 80 % crosslinkers is used [96]. Commercially available crosslinkers include ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) and trimethylolpropane trimethacrylate (TRIM) [97]. It is usually observed that a high concentration of vinyl units in the crosslinkers produces strong binding and high mechanical stability in MIPs. A high amount of vinyl units was observed while using ethylene glycol trimethacrylate and trimethylolpropane trimethacrylate in MIPs preparation [98–104].

The primary role of the solvent in developing MIPs is to organize the template, functional monomers, and crosslinkers into a single-phase [105,106]. Another crucial contribution of the solvent is the generation of macro-porous structures. The choice of porogenic solvent ultimately affects the production of uniform pores. If a thermodynamically favorable solvent is used, a macro-porous structure is formed with defined surface areas [107–111]. While a thermodynamically non-favorable solvent results in the production of a small number of pores with small volume. Apart from these considerations, aprotic and non-polar solvents can be used for the synthesis of the non-covalent imprinted. It is noted that the solvent affects the template and functional monomer interaction, imprinting efficiency, sorption, and morphology of the final polymer [112].

Using excess solvent increases the pore size and generates well-designed cavities in the template [113–118]. The functional monomer stabilization can be improved while using less polar solvents in terms of complex formation with hydrogen bond stabilization. In the case of hydrophobic forces-driven complexation of the components, polar solvents like water could be used. Chloroform, acetonitrile, dichloroethane, methanol, dimethylformamide, toluene, tetrahydrofuran, and 2-methoxy ethanol are common porogenic solvents [119].

The selection of the initiator to start the polymerization depends on the reaction conditions. If the template is sensitive to thermal or photochemical stimuli, then in such a case, a thermally or photochemically driven initiator must be avoided [120,121]. Another scenario is that if hydrogen bonding is driving the complexation of the polymerizing components, then, the temperature of the system must be lowered. In such a case, a photochemically driven initiator is chosen so that the complexation can occur under low temperatures.

The most common initiators in the free radical polymerization synthesis of MIPs are azo and peroxide compounds [122]. Fig. 5 displays some of the initiators. Azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN), for example, requires a high temperature for decomposition and is an effective initiator in carbon-centered radical polymerization [123]. Free radical polymerization requires an inert atmosphere to effectively proceed without the oxygen side reactions.

The active working part of MIPs is their binding sites located inside MIPs cavities, which play a major role in capturing the analytes [124]. The number of binding sites can vary from a few thousand to millions depending on the size of MIPs. This is an additional benefit of MIPs compared with the biological receptors that only possess a single or a few binding sites. Because the location of the binding sites plays a major role in MIPs and template interaction, it is necessary to conceive the location and features of the binding sites. The number of binding sites has a direct effect on the activity of MIPs. The integrity and stability of the binding sites depend on the crosslinkers and tend to stabilize the binding sites to increase the diffusion rate of the approaching analytes [125].

The pore size distribution also affects the imprinting, which is controlled by the porogenic solvent. The pore size distribution is associated with the diffusional mass transfer and the varying swelling degrees. Depending on the stated factors, different-sized binding sites could be obtained with varying degrees of accessibility by the analyte [126]. If the size of the binding sites lies in the meso-macro porous range (>20 Å) it is easily accessed by the analyte, while that in the microporous range (<20 Å) has lower diffusion [127,128]. One of the important factors is the swelling of MIPs, which alters the density and approaches the limit of the binding sites. While considering the accessibility of the binding sites for the analyte molecules, it is noticed that the sites located superficially on MIPs surface are more accessible than the intrinsic ones. In the case of bulk polymerization, the intrinsic number of binding sites is high which results in poor rebinding [129]. To obtain enough exposed binding sites, a modified strategy could be followed which involves the synthesis of MIPs on the surface of solid supports like nanoparticles to improve the analytes rebinding [130].

The synthesis of MIPs can follow any of the multiple polymerization mechanisms. The components of polymerization include monomers, crosslinkers, initiators, and templates, which interact with each other via different mechanisms. These polymerization mechanisms include free radical (FRP) [131], controlled radical (CRP) which may include atom transfer radical (ATRP), reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT), nitroxide-mediated (NMP), and iniferter-mediated [132–135].

Free radical polymerization (FRP) is the most followed mechanism for polymer synthesis at the industrial scale such as in plastics. This polymerization is equally important in MIPs synthesis [136]. The vinyl-based monomers are well suited for carrying out the FRP. Among these groups are styrene, ethylene, and methyl methacrylate. The polymers obtained following FRP are highly branched, have a wide range of molecular weights, and have atactic structures [137]. FRP usually operates with vinyl monomers, which are available commercially and cheap. The conditions required for carrying out FRP are mild. These reasons make FRP the most followed method for MIPs synthesis, Fig. 6
[138,139].

Broadly speaking, FRP is classified into three steps, (i) initiation, (ii) propagation, and (iii) termination. The polymerization starts with the initiation step, which produces free radicals. The production of the free radicals commences with an external source like heat or light irradiation [140]. These free radicals remain active during the entire course of the polymerization and interact with the vinyl functional monomers to generate the primary free radical [141]. The primary free radical further propagates the polymerization entering the propagation step. The propagation step generates long-chain growth of MIPs by the addition of the monomers. The propagation step is also known as the rate-determining step of polymerization. A general observation is that the rate of propagation is faster than the rate of initiation. This could further be explained as the polymerization starts, propagation also begins within a second or two.

Polymerization could also be terminated by the combination of two monomers. The termination step may occur via combination or disproportionation of two radicals. In the case of combination polymerization, the two radicals combine producing a single non-reactive polymer chain. While in the case of disproportionation, a hydrogen atom transfer occurs between two radicals resulting in the formation of two polymers, one of which bears a saturated end while the other bears an unsaturated end, both containing an initiator fragment [142]. One of the important considerations while performing FRP is the oxygen-free environment because oxygen presence slows down the FRP process. The inert atmosphere ensures the high reproducibility of polymers. The removal of dissolved oxygen from the monomers before initiating the polymerization could be performed by ultrasonication. An alternate way is purging an inert gas such as nitrogen or argon. For more efficient degassing, the freeze–pumpthaw method could also be used. Although FRP is a usually used polymerization technique, some of its disadvantages include irreversible termination reactions and uncontrolled atom transfer [143]. Due to this reason, control over the growth of the polymer chain and its termination becomes difficult. To overcome such issues, more advanced methods could be applied with control over the number, size, and structure of polymer chains [144–146].

An alternative to FRP is controlled radical polymerization (CRP) [147]. Compared with FRP, CRP offers faster activation-deactivation cycles [148]. The different types of CRP include ATRP [149], RAFT [150], NMP [151], and iniferter-mediated polymerization [152]. The most frequently followed techniques are ATRP and RAFT polymerization mechanisms (Fig. 7 ). Polymers produced from these techniques are monodispersed, have uniform morphology, and are sensitive to the targeted analytes. These techniques can be applied with a variety of photo-initiators and functional monomers [153,154]. In the case of ATRP, the radicals could be produced by cleaving the carbon-halogen bond by central metal oxidation. Some vinyl monomers could be used in ATRP including styrene, acrylates, and methacrylate. In the case of ATRP, a suitable central metal atom, its associated ligands, and halogens must be chosen carefully to have complete control over polymerization [155]. An elevated temperature in the range of 80–150 °C is usually required for ATRP. ATRP has become the preferred technique for different polymer synthesis including graft polymer brushes [156]. Another technique of CRP is RAFT [157]. RAFT is a more flexible strategy for obtaining polymers. RAFT mechanism has been utilized in precipitation and bulk polymerizations to synthesize MIPs [158]. However, a high rate of control over the size of the polymer results in reduced heterogeneity, non-stereospecificity, and formation of atactic structures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms based on different initiation sources
ce:section.ce:para: Various studies on different mechanisms of initiation for MIPs synthesis were conducted. These sources of initiation include thermal [159], photo-generated [160], microwave-assisted [161], and electrochemical inputs [162]. Among the stated initiating strategies, photo-initiated polymerization has frequently been used (Fig. 8 ).

Photopolymerization requires a UV source to start polymerization. This initiation usually results in the fabrication of thin films at the nanoscale range. The photo-initiators operate dually. In one of the scenarios, photo-initiators absorb UV or visible light and produce free radicals by homolytic bond cleavage [163]. This type of initiation is observed in benzoin ether-based, diazo, acetophenone, and acyl phosphine oxide derivatives. While in the second photoinitiation mechanism, there is an exchange of electrons/protons of hydrogen between the co-initiator and photosensitizer. The photosensitizers that are frequently used are thioxanthones, benzophenones, and anthraquinones. While the respective co-initiators include methyl diethanolamine, ethyl p-dimethylaminobenzoate, and N-phenyl glycine all of which contain labile hydrogen [164]. Although photopolymerization emerged as the most used class of FRP, the limitation associated with this source is that penetration of light becomes difficult in the opaque medium. Also, light cannot penetrate completely while using macromolecules [165].

Another type is electropolymerization which is used for the development of MIPs films onto the electrodes for sensing applications [166]. MIPs electrodeposition can be performed using cathodic or anodic electropolymerization [167]. MIPs-based sensors are synthesized using spin casting [168]. Other advantages of electropolymerization include high reproducibility and convenient removal of the template by over-oxidation. Fouling of the electrodes by adsorption of the byproducts is a major drawback. This challenge could be overcome by using an electro-inactive analog or deposition of a barrier layer beneath MIPs. Electropolymerization generates disordered chains attributed to the fact that upon polymerization completion, the solvated counter ions may be in constant motion [169]. The expansion and contraction of polymer films result in the modification of polymer volume. This problem could be overcome using electropolymerization having doped counter ions to counteract the charge and to isolate well-ordered and charged polymers [170].

Another useful scheme is to utilize dual monomers to obtain distinctive features of both monomers, i.e., copolymerization [138]. The copolymer could be obtained using two types of vinyl polymers such as methyl methacrylate and butyl methacrylate. Copolymers have distributive features of both monomers with a long-chain structure [171]. The obtained structure has alternate units of both monomers with a statistical distribution depending on the relative concentration of both polymers in the reaction. The production of MIPs requires specific conditions considering the relative reactivity of each monomer. In some instances, the monomer pairs do not interact such as maleic anhydride and stilbene [172]. The best interaction pair could be chosen based on the reactivity ratios of monomers. The reactivity and rate of propagation depend on various experimental factors and the concentration of monomers [173].

Although the preparatory practice of MIPs seems simple, a detailed study showed that multiple factors affect their synthesis. The preparation of MIPs is rather complex that is influenced by a few variables [174] (Fig. 9 ). The essence of molecular memory associated with MIPs depends on the template-monomers adducts formed before polymerization. These template-monomer adducts interact with different types of strengths [175]. The number of binding sites formed because of polymerization and their heterogeneity depends on the binding equilibrium (ΔGbind) of the self-assembled template-monomer adducts. The stronger the template-monomer adduct interaction, the larger the number of binding sites. Template-monomer coordination depends on the nature of the components in the mixture and the reaction conditions [176]. Equilibrium between template and monomers is a result of the effect of pressure and temperature. Low temperature favors polymerization by building strong electrostatic interactions, while elevated temperatures provide polymers with promising features. Similarly, high pressure of 1000 bar results in advanced selectivity of MIPs [177,178].

Different methods were followed for the removal of the template from MIPs structure. The template removal methods depend on the strength between the template and functional monomers [179]. Simple washing using detergents, different solvents, Soxhlet method, ultrasound treatment, excess ions or molecules, adding digesting enzymes like proteases, heating, or acids/bases could result in template removal [180–183]. The problem in such cases is that such molecules are attached to MIPs and block the binding sites. Template removal is an important aspect of MIPs synthesis. If the template remains inside the interstices of MIPs, this leads to “template bleeding”, which refers to the slow liberation of the template from MIPs [184].

Cavities are formed when the template incorporated in MIPs during polymerization is removed [185]. This cavity is an imprint of the template, which makes it specific for the analyte (template) of interest [186]. Thus, imprinting is a key factor in obtaining analyte-specific MIPs. The interpretation of the imprinting of MIPs depends on the interaction of the template with the functional monomers in the solvent [187]. One of the common strategies for evaluating the imprinting effect is to prepare the non-imprinted polymer (NIP) which is like that of MIPs except for the fact that it does not contain any template-specific cavities [188].

NIPs are a twin of MIPs and are synthesized following the same strategy and under the same conditions used for MIPs. The only difference is that the template is not added during NIPs synthesis [189]. NIPs could be used to compare MIPs imprinting effect and specificity toward the analyte [190]. The sensitivity of MIPs is higher toward the template compared with NIPs due to the presence of template-shaped cavities with numerous binding sites [191] (Fig. 10 ). This comparative study could be performed using sorption and chromatographic techniques. While performing the comparative study using sorption, the binding studies are performed on MIPs and NIPs. The equally weighted mass of MIPs and NIPs was taken in separate vials along with the analyte solution [192]. The sorbed analyte amount is determined for both MIPs and NIPs. The obtained results showed higher sorption efficiency of MIPs compared with NIPs. Another study for the interpretation of cavities is the chromatographic approach [193]. MIPs and NIPs were embedded into the chromatographic column and the analyte solution was drained through the column until the saturation point was attained [194]. Analysis of the results indicated higher specificity toward the analyte for MIPs. Further accuracy in the imprinting analysis of MIPs was obtained by calculating the imprinting factor (IF). IF is obtained by the retention of the targeted analyte in packed column chromatography with a given stationary phase [195]. IF calculation is also a means of MIPs characterization before using in different applications [196]. Other frequently employed forms of MIPs include MIPs-based films and membranes [197], micro monoliths [198], and microfabricated MIPs [199].

Computational studies provide a rationale for MIPs design at the molecular level regarding the template-monomer interaction, factors affecting polymerization, and synthesis of MIPs with specific properties [200–203]. Quantum mechanical simulations of the statistical treatment methods could perform theoretical and computational studies about the design of MIPs. In the pre-polymerization step, quantum mechanical calculations are performed based on the description of the electronic structure of the interacting molecules [204]. It gives a rough estimate of the interaction level in the template-monomers complex. It also aids in choosing a suitable functional monomer for the template of interest by providing the binding energy values [205]. Although density field theory (DFT) has been frequently used for achieving excellent results, the drawback associated with this strategy is the difficulty in handling many atoms [206]. In addition, the choice of a specific solvent is another problem while performing these calculations. Such problems could be tackled by using the polarizable continuum model (PCM) methodology [207]. Molecular dynamics are based on Newtonian laws of motion, classical methods are incorporated for each atom in the simulated system. This method can be employed for the empirical study of large molecules. Nevertheless, the problem associated with this analysis is the low accuracy compared with that of the quantum or semi-empirical approaches [208]. Thus, a combined approach of using the quantum mechanics-molecular mechanics method (QM/MM) is being employed which allows the simulation of small parts of the system using quantum mechanics while the large parts could be handled via molecular dynamics [209].

Another form of computational study is statistical and mathematical analysis, which is termed chemometrics and is performed on the available chemical data. This helps in extracting the optimized conditions and parameters such as the amount of the components, interacting ratios, temperature, and rebinding conditions for carrying out the synthesis of MIPs [210,211]. The different multitude of techniques for statistical analysis includes analysis of variance (ANOVA), which is a univariate approach to optimizing a single parameter and then using that parameter for the optimization of the next one [212]. Another approach used for data classification is principal component analysis (PCA). Other methods used for multivariate calibration include multiple linear regressions (MLR), principal component regression (PCR) and partial least squares regression (PLSR), and artificial neural networking (ANN) [213,214].

An important feature of MIPs is their morphology [215]. While MIPs synthesis follows copolymerization, whether the reaction medium contains a single monomer or multiple monomers, the synthesis is affected by two major parameters. The structural elucidation could be made first by knowing the ratio of the crosslinker to the number of moles of the functional monomer and second is the solvent volume [216]. Although other parameters also influence the final form of MIPs, these two factors are likely to control the morphology of MIPs. Information about crosslinkers, functional monomers ratio, and solvent volume can predict the final form of MIPs [217]. All forms of MIPs could be obtained by changing the crosslinker-monomer ratios and solvent volume [218].

In a generalized scenario, gel polymers are formed when the phase separation does not occur [219]. This gel-type polymer becomes amorphous once it is dried. This polymer exhibits small surface areas due to the close interaction of the molecules and possesses a high swelling capacity when exposed to the solvent. These features of gel polymers render them mechanically unstable [220]. The alternate approach is achieved by increasing the crosslinker ratio and solvent volume. Under such conditions, phase separation occurs during polymerization. This results in the development of macro-porous polymers of highly porous structures with large surface areas [221]. A more advanced condition arises when the solvent volume exceeds the normal limit. The highly dilute solvent medium keeps the polymer particles apart which gives rise to microgel powder [222]. Precipitation polymerization emerged as an interesting method in the production of MIPs in the micro-size range [223].

Several techniques are used to determine the chemical composition of MIPs [224,225]. The characterization techniques for the chemical analysis of MIPs include elemental microanalysis [226], Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) [227], and solid-state NMR [228]. The elemental analysis provides an estimate of the percentage of hydrogen, carbon, nitrogen, and other elements [229]. A suitable technique for monitoring the functional groups in MIPs is FTIR. The non-covalent interactions like hydrogen bonding, taking place inside MIPs can also be evaluated by FTIR spectra [230].

Several techniques are available for the structural analysis of MIPs [231]. The most important aspects in the structural illustration of MIPs include specific surface area, S (m2/g), specific pore volume, Vp (mL/g), average pore diameter, Dp (Å), and pore size distribution, dVp/dDp. The technique is usually employed for the structural analysis and porosity of MIPs including solvent uptake experiments, nitrogen sorption porosimetry, mercury intrusion porosimetry, inverse size exclusion chromatography, and scanning electron microscopy [232]. Solvent uptake experiments are performed to determine the specific pore volume on MIPs surface. Nitrogen sorption porosimetry follows Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) theory and provides information about “S”, Vp, Dp (Å), and dVp/dDp for MIPs [233]. Based on these calculations, a general specification of the size range can be assigned using the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) rules. In addition, mercury intrusion porosimetry gives information about the porous network with a focus on macro-pores. Inverse size-exclusion chromatography is used on MIPs in wet [234] (Fig. 11 ). The most frequently used techniques for accumulating information about the structure of MIPs are light and scanning electron microscopy [235].
Synthesis of MIPs can be performed using a few methods [236]. Table 1 presents different synthesis methods of MIPs.

The process is rather simple, which consumes the functional monomer, template, initiator, crosslinker, and porogenic solvent at once [237]. The obtained MIPs must be sieved and ground. Although the method has been frequently used, the post-treatment like sieving and grinding is time-consuming. Another issue is that the prepared polymer particles are irregular in shape and size [238]. Furthermore, the mechanical handling of the polymer may destroy the adsorption sites and binding groups. Bulk polymerization is known for its poor selectivity and reproducibility [239].

Another method is suspension or single-step polymerization, which often results in the formation of spherical-shaped polymer particles [240]. The problem associated with this method is the large size particles in the micrometer-millimeter range. Water can be used in the synthesis, but for the continuous phase, preparation in mineral oil and perfluorocarbon is possible. Suspension polymerization is the only reported method that can be applied for the large-scale preparation of MIPs [241,242].

Another method for the large-scale synthesis of monodispersed MIPs is emulsion polymerization. It is specified for working in dual media such as water-in-oil and oil-in-water. Initially, the functional monomers and the template formed an emulsified mixture in the presence of a surfactant [243]. The initiator was also present in the continuous phase. The monomers are coated with the surfactant to form micelles in the continuous phase with a diameter range of 2–10 nm. The initiating reagent penetrates the surface of the micelles to reach the monomers producing spherical polymers with a size range of 10–100 nm [244–247]. The obtained polymers exhibit electrostatic repulsion with other polymers, which inhibits the aggregation. The useful features of MIPs obtained by emulsion polymerization include submicroscopic level, water-swollen, and hydrophilic properties. Although emulsion polymerization emerged as a useful method, the surfactant presence may cause potential toxicity [248].

One of the polymerization methods for the large-scale synthesis of spherical MIPs particles in the size range of 50–100 nm in a single step is precipitation polymerization [249]. It requires many template molecules. Furthermore, precipitation polymerization involves the synthesis in a dilute solution of monomers and the whole process is surfactant-free [250]. Many factors affect the particle size such as solvent polarity, stirring speed, and reaction temperature [251,252].

Multi-step swelling polymerization produces a uniform and spherical polymer particle in a well-monodispersed size and shape. It allows the formation of uniform beads in controlled diameter [253]. MIPs prepared by this method exhibited multi-sweep swelling and are used in chromatographic applications. The realization of multi-step swelling polymerization is connected to the preparation of aqueous emulsions, which influenced the imprinting and selectivity of MIPs [254]. The drawback of the multi-step swelling polymerization is the complications of polymerization and the long time consumed for the reaction to complete.

Another form of molecular imprinting is the surface imprinting method. This imprinting accounts for developing the binding surfaces onto the polymer surface. This property gives the benefit of providing a feasible approach for the targeted molecule to the binding surfaces by minimizing the distance and the steric hindrance [255]. This polymerization is carried out by grafting a thin layer of MIPs onto a solid support such as silica or nanoparticles. To obtain a better surface imprinting of the targeted molecule, a pre-functionalization of the polymer can be performed. The functionalization helps to immobilize the polymer onto the surface grafted to. The drawback associated with this polymerization is a long time consumed [256,257].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Covalent and non-covalent imprinting in MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: The important feature of MIPs is their selective recognition of the targeted analytes. The binding ability of MIPs depends on their morphology, chemical nature, and flexibility. Apart from these factors, the template-monomer interactions influence the binding, rebinding, kinetics, and thermodynamics of the system [285]. The best quality of MIPs can be obtained when the desorption is fast, displays satisfactory rebinding kinetics, and demonstrates high specificity. These factors depend on the type and nature of the interactions between the template and functional monomers. The different types of interactions offer selectivity and reversibility exhibited by MIPs [286] (Fig. 12 ). The interaction between the template and the functional monomer must be strong enough to be fixed in a specific orientation. In addition, complete template removal must be ensured after polymerization. While evaluating the applications of MIPs, the prime factor is the interaction of the functional groups on the binding sites with the targeted analyte. The interaction must be reversible and rapid to ensure good applications in chromatography and catalysis for example. This requires high activation energy for the binding function, while the other functions may require low energy [287,288]. A closer look at the literature shows multiple interactions between the functional monomers and the template. These interactions may be covalent, non-covalent interactions, semi-covalent, or metal ion coordination [289].

The interactions are developed between the functional groups of the template with the functional monomers followed by copolymerization with the crosslinker. The covalent bond formation follows the copolymerization with the crosslinker to yield MIPs [290]. After the process completion, the imprinted molecule is released from MIPs cage by chemical cleavage. The most observed covalent bondings include boronic ester, Schiff base, and ketal formation. Template removal is viable in the case of boronic ester and Schiff bases, however, hard in ketal bonds [291]. In the case of the ketal, the binding and substrate release are even harder [291]. However, boronic acid-based polymers show a fast rate of rearrangement from trigonal planar to tetrahedral at boron upon ammonia addition. Under these conditions, equilibrium is achieved faster. Ketal and Schiff bases formation can be enhanced by the addition of acid. Covalent bonds provide high specificity and directionality [292]. The covalent interaction requires more specific conditions like ratios of crosslinkers, functional monomers, and templates compared with non-covalent interactions. Due to the limited choices of templates available for the covalent interactions with the monomers and requiring acid hydrolysis for template removal, they are less common [293]. In addition, the covalent interactions offer a slow rebinding kinetics rate.

Another approach for building MIPs is the non-covalent, which follows the mixing of the components to obtain MIPs. In this approach, the monomers interact with the template via hydrogen bonding, ionic interactions, and electrostatic non-covalent interactions [294]. The rebinding of the analyte with the cavities of MIPs also follows the same type of interaction. The non-covalent interaction provides a better chance for template extraction, hence, providing cavities, and the rebinding is thus faster. Although this interaction offers a facile approach for imprinting, it has low association constants [295]. The complete equilibrium transfer toward binding can be attained using excess monomers. However, this approach may cause the production of non-specific binding sites inside the polymer matrix. Another anomaly associated with this approach is that 15 % of cavities show that the rebinding of the template is losing 85 % of cavities because of shrinking during the rebinding [296].

A hybrid approach of imprinting involves covalent interactions, meanwhile, rebinding can be achieved via a non-covalent interaction. The covalent interactions offer exact stoichiometry and stability while non-covalent interactions provide a rapid equilibration [297–299].

While the bindings could be covalent or non-covalent, both types have their pros and cons [300]. In the case of multiple binding sites, the best interactions between the functional monomers and the template could be obtained if the components of MIPs are directional. This property can be achieved by following the covalent interactions between the monomers and the template. While in the case of the non-covalent interactions, some bonding interactions offer directionality in MIPs synthesis. One such type of interaction is metal–ligand coordination [301] (Fig. 13 ). The idea of metal–ligand coordination originated from the fact that the naturally occurring metal ions show excellent compatibility with the biological ligands. This idea was utilized for MIPs design to obtain binding sites. The bonding provides high versatility toward the targeted functional groups, good rebinding kinetics, and strong individual interactions [302,303].

Commercially available sensors have short life spans. This shortcoming has been tackled using MIPs-based sensors, which are robust and selective with a low limit of quantification (LOQ) and limit of detection (LOD). The different MIPs-based sensors and their applications are discussed below [304,305].

Capacitance sensors [306], chemical sensors [307], and field-effect transistors [308] based on MIPs can explain the general measurement methods and sensor implementation techniques for analyte recognition. These sensors require dielectric materials to fabricate the electrodes. The conductive component is made from MIPs composites, which include metallic nanoparticles [309], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [310], or any conductive polymer [311]. The conductive component generates a conductive layer (reporting layer) above the electrodes. Most gas phase samples are investigated by the conductometric sensing method [312].

Capacitive sensors are composed of a conductive sandwich, which includes dielectric MIPs, a conductive layer on an insulating substrate, and a solid lattice-like electrode [313]. Fig. 14
explains the circuit of the capacitive sensor. The structure shows that the electronic monitor and the two electrodes are mutually connected. The electronic monitor is a simple meter or an impedance measuring chip. The template adsorption on MIPs effectively changes both the sensor dissipation factor and capacitance [314].

Chemiresistors are easy to fabricate. Here, we only need a layer of a single electrode, which may be a pair of simple electrodes with a polymer filling [315,316]. The empty area between the electrodes or an interdigitated electrode (IDE) configuration with about 160 pairs of sophisticated electrodes, and the coating of MIPs in the entire IDE.In the case of chemiresistors, the important part is MIPs in connection with a conductive film, which can be constructed using conductive polymers such as polypyrrole, polyaniline, and poly[3,4-(ethylenedioxy)thiophene] as MIPs host [317]. In the case of recording electronics, it is known that the targeted molecule adsorption and desorption may cause resistance to the change in the recording. To measure the resistance change, the use of a Wheatstone bridge or simple ohmmeter or the use of constant current along with voltage measurement may be highly effective [318,319].

Chemicalfield-effect transistors (CHEMFET) offer higher sensitivity compared with chemiresistor [320].In CHEMFET, there are three electrodes for the sensor, i.e., drain, source, and gate, while the gate is coated by MIPs [321]. The potential is produced on the gate and the net voltage is affected by the template adsorption–desorption and by the transistor electrical conductance [322]. Like a chemiresistor, there should be a conductive polymer for the construction of conductive materials.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy produces high-quality sensing which measures the electrical parameters and generates changes on the surface of the electrode [323]. Here, MIPs and the working electrodes record the current as a function of oscillation potential in a wide range of frequencies. The impedance is an equivalent circuit standard that includes surface capacitance and resistance. These parameters are the result of the targeted molecule adsorption on the thin film, which generates the calibration curve and the actual sample analysis. In the case of targeted molecule adsorption, LOD depends on the targeted molecule and experimental conditions [324,325].

Quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) is useful for sensing liquid and gas samples [326]. The sensors offer real-time sensing because of MIPs coating. QCM is composed of a thin layer of piezoelectric quartz crystal, which is sandwiched between metal electrodes. After the application of AC which creates stress, the crystal starts vibration under resonant frequency [327]. This resonant frequency shows variation due to the adsorption of targeted molecules on the surface of MIPs which increases the crystal total mass as shown in Eq. (1).
[1]
Δ m = - C Δ f
whereΔm is mass increase and C is mass sensitivity constant (ng cm−2 Hz−1), and the change in resonant frequency is Δf. The oscillation circuit uses microbalance as a network analyzer or as a frequency counter to monitor the change in frequency while the intermediate layer is used to increase MIPs adhesion and for the baseline frequency change with an increase in the mass [328–330].

Electrochemical sensors contain three electrodes and initially were used for sensing liquid samples in a cell [331]. An amperometric method is used in current-based measurement under constant potential, and it is a potentiometric method if the measurement is under varying potentials [332]. In either case, the peak current, as the voltage is scanned, is proportional to the concentration of the targeted molecule. The concept of the two methods is the same although the variation of numbers in both methods must be astounding [333–336].

Additional sensors include MIPs-coated [337], surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [338], and optical MIPs [339]. The crystals of SPR are used for the detection of the targeted molecules on the surface of imprinted polymers. The working procedure of plasmon sensors is the oscillation of all conduction electrons between those materials having two different dielectric constants, and there is a thin layer of MIPs on the sensor crystals and a metal (gold or silver) [340]. In addition, the incident radiations must be used for the stimulation of the resonance frequency. Template adsorption on MIPs changes the refractive index and the plasmon resonance frequency [341]. Optical MIPs sensors are designated as molecularly imprinted optosensing materials (MIOMs) and can be used for liquid and gas samples. Optical MIPs sensors rely on the quantum dots and function by fluorescence quenching as the active elements. The coating is used for the activation of the quantum dot, which is then utilized in reaction, and further leads to the imprinted polymer layer, and adding MIPs specificity may not be removed from the quantum dots fluorescence [342]. The decrease in signals is used for monitoring the binding efficiency and reported 93 % quenching [299].Fluorescence sensitivity measurement is special for optical MIPs sensors with LOD of 44 nM [343].Some examples of optical MIPs sensors are mentioned in Table 2 .

As discussed in the previous section, MIPs-based sensors have many applications in quantitative and qualitative analysis. These sensors improved the quality of products in different industries like food, pharmaceutics, agriculture, and horticulture.

A review of the available literature about gas sensing applications of MIPs-based sensors shows that multiple sensors could be fabricated (Fig. 15 ) [372]. The conductive MIPs-based sensor was fabricated at the surface of a gold-coated optical fiber via electropolymerization [373]. The working electrode consists of a metal film on the surface of which the polymer could grow. The fiber core of the sensor was formerly photoengraved having tilted fiber Bragg grating so that its surrounding refractive index sensitivity could be considered. The sensor was used for sensing formaldehyde vapors. The results showed high selectivity of the polymer-coated sensor toward formaldehyde with a sensitivity of 2.10 pm/ppm showing a linear behavior and LOD of a few ppm. Also, gas sensing applications of a 300 MHz surface acoustic wave gas sensor coated with MIPs were analyzed [374]. Coated MIPs were synthesized with Sarin acid as the template and o-phenylenediamine as the functional monomer using co-polymerization using cyclic voltammetry. The fabricated sensor was used for the detection of dimethyl-methyl-phosphonate with a sensitivity of 96 Hz/mg/m3 and LOD of 0.5 mg/m3.

Another successful study was performed for the detection of methanol gas using a MIPs-based sensor [375]. The sensing material 1/99Ag-LaFeO3 was prepared by a combination of the sol–gel method and microwave chemical synthesis [376,377]. MIPs were prepared using methanol as the template and methyl acrylic acid as the functional monomer. The crosslinker LaFeO3 and the radical initiator AIBN were also added. In the next step, the sensor was prepared by coating MIPs on the surface of the alumina tube with Au electrodes and Pt wires. The sensors were then used for the recognition of methanol gas at optimized temperature conditions in the range of 70–170 °C to achieve the highest response of 41 obtained at 130 °C. LaFeO3 is a typical p-type semiconductor in air and its gas uptake mechanism depends on the resistance before and after exposure to the targeted gas. The ionization of La3+ causes the formation of vacancies and charge-carrier holes (h*). The oxygen present in the air could be captured by the material surface to produce O- ads and O2- ads by seizing the free electrons. This produces a thin space-charge layer, reducing the potential barrier and lowering the resistance of the sensor. MIPs-coating onto the sensor further increases the sensing properties due to the selectivity and sensitivity of MIPs toward methanol, which could be attributed to the presence of methanol-shaped cavities inside MIPs. The sensor showed a response and recovery time of 40 s and 50 s, respectively.

A study was conducted on formaldehyde (HCHO) vapor detection [378]. The chemical sensor consisted of polypyrrole-based MIPs (PPy-based MIPs) synthesized on the titanium dioxide nanotube array (TiO2-NTA). MIPs incorporation increases the surface-to-volume ratio of the nanotube with MIPs being the best sensing material. The sensor was developed by TiO2-NTA preparation via anodic oxidation of a titanium sheet, followed by electropolymerization [373] of MIPs onto its surface. The prepared sensor showed a strong response of 13 % toward HCHO vapors with a 1 ppm concentration at room temperature. This could be explained by the fact that the PPy-based MIPs layer is acting as an intrinsic n-type doped semiconductor. The HCHO complementary cavities of PPy-based MIPs distinguish the targeted analyte from other interfering species due to the difference in monomer functionalities and molecular sizes. The exposure of PPy-based MIPs to HCOH causes the uptake of formaldehyde by developing bonding where HCOH donates electrons to MIPs. This causes an increased conductance of the semiconductor through conjugation [379] and swelling effects [380], which increases the charge transfer and mobility, ultimately increasing the conductance. Table 3 contains some of the gas probing applications of MIPs-based sensors.

Researchers [394] developed MIPs-based surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) sensors for the removal of sibutramine (SIB), an illegal addiction drug. The magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers (MMIPs) were prepared in two steps. First is the solvothermal synthesis [395] of the magnetic nanoparticles followed by the synthesis of MIPs onto the surface of NPs via copolymerization. MMIPs were then used as SERS-active substrates for sensing SIB drugs. The results showed a rapid and ultrasensitive detection of SIB with LOD of 1.0 × 10-9 M. Another group [396] developed biomimetic potentiometric ion-selective electrodes (ISEs) based on MIPs sensors. The prepared sensors were used for the analysis of amino acridine in pharmaceutical and biological samples. The sensors were labeled Ⅰ and Ⅱ based on the differences in functional monomers (methacrylic acid or acrylamide). The results showed a high sensitivity toward the targeted drug with LOD of 0.05 and 0.17 μg/mL for sensors I and II, respectively (Fig. 16 ).

Potentiometric sensors based on the highly selective MIPs for the reversible detection of neutral dopamine were fabricated [397]. MIPs of the sensor was synthesized using vinyl borate as the functional monomer. The chemistry of the functional monomer could be explained based on the functional groups, which could easily be stimulated by pH and develop a covalent interaction with dopamine forming a five-membered cyclic ester. When the ISEs are exposed to dopamine concentrations, the dopamine molecules are distributed between the aqueous phase and the organic phase depending on MIPs membrane’s selective recognition sites. In the next step, the complex interacts with quaternary ammonium salts (R4N+) to form the associated ion complex MIPs/dopamine−/R4N+ ion. The formed H+ and X- ions move from the membrane toward the aqueous phase showing an anionic response. The unexpected anionic response is attributed to the hydrogen ions movement because of the interaction of phenolic compounds with quaternary ammonium salts present in the membrane. The results showed a good response of solid contact ISE based on man-tailored mimics for the potentiometric transduction of neutral dopamine with LOD of 0.15 µM and a fast response time (<10 s).

Another study was conducted [398] to design capacitive sensors based on MIPs for the detection of benzyl methyl ketone (BMK). The process was initiated by using 1-vinylimidazole and itaconic acid as functional monomers. The material was then immobilized onto the surface of gold transducers using tyramine as a crosslinker. The electrochemical sensor showed a satisfactory response toward BMK detection with LOD value of 1 µM. Good reproducibility of the electrochemical sensor strengthened its activity. Table 4 contains some of the pharmaceutical applications of MIPs-based sensors.

Chemicals and biological toxins like additives, packaging materials, pesticides, raw food materials, fungi, and bacteria can cause foodborne illnesses. To avoid toxicity by food products, proper screening and sensing of the food products is necessary to ensure their quality. MIPs-based sensors have put extraordinary, efficient, and promising results due to their selectivity nature. A group of researchers [427] designed a laser-grafted MIPs-based sensor for the detection of histamine, biogenic amine (BA) often found in food products. The MIPs-based sensor was synthesized via the metal-free photo atom transfer radical polymerization grafting [428]. The prepared sensor showed an excellent response toward histamine with LOD of 3.4 nM (Fig. 17 ).

Magnetic MIPs-based electrochemical sensors were prepared for the detection of Gram-negative bacterial quorum signaling molecules (N-acyl-homoserine-lactones) [429]. In the first step, magnetic MIPs was synthesized by the surface imprinting method followed by deposition onto the magnetic carbon paste electrode (MGCE) surface. The electrochemical measurements for sensing the AHLs were performed using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) to obtain the oxidative current signal. The results showed an excellent response of the developed sensor toward AHLs with LOD of 8 × 10−10 mol/L.

Carbon dots-filter paper combined with a MIPs film sensor was developed [430]. The prepared sensor was then utilized for the determination of 3-chloropropane-1,2-diol (3-MCPD) which is a food adulterant posing carcinogenic effects. It is mostly found in soy sauce, infant foods, meat products, etc. The study showed that the grafted paper-based sensor has an excellent sensing property toward 3-MCPD with LOD of 0.6 ng m/L. The mechanism of the interaction between MIPs and 3-MCPD incorporates hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic forces. A novel smartphone-based optical biomimetic sensor utilizing free-standing MIPs was developed [431]. The developed sensor was used for the detection of aflatoxin B1. The targeted analyte of the developed optical sensors is toxic causing carcinogenic and mutagenic effects. The designed MIPs-based sensor can detect aflatoxin B1 and produce a fluorometric sensor response that can be easily analyzed using the smartphone. The sensing LOD for aflatoxin B1 is 20 ng m/L. Table 5 lists some of the food analysis applications using MIPs-based sensors.

A unique type of quantification involves the distinction between the chiral molecules through the enantio-differentiating signals produced by MIPs-based sensors. The chiral molecule recognition properties are fundamental because each enantiomer produces different properties in pharmacological or biological systems. The chiral sensors based on MIPs offer three-dimensional active sites for the selective recognition of the enantiomers [459]. A group of scientists [460] investigated the chiral recognition of l-amino acids. The basic concept of this operation was to develop a chiral sensor based on the single-template molecular imprinting method. The developed sensor was then employed for the specific recognition of D/l-alanine (D/l-Ala) and D/l-tyrosine (D/l-Tyr). The sensor was developed by hydrolyzing l-alanyl-l-tyrosine dipeptide doped in silica/polypyrrole (SiO2/PPy) in an acidic system, forming l-Tyr and l-Ala co-imprinted chiral sensor. The factors like rebinding time and rebinding potential, which affect the recognition of the chiral enantiomers, were also optimized. The increase in potential (0.1–0.5 V) caused an increase in the recognition of Tyr enantiomer due to increased electrostatic interaction with the sensor. Moreover, the developed chiral sensors have found immense applications in the field of amino acid separations (Fig. 18 ).

Another group [461] prepared norepinephrine-coated magnetic MIPs-based sensors for enantioselective recognition of amino acids. The first step was the synthesis of MIPs utilizing norepinephrine as the functional monomer, and template of choice (l-tryptophan, l-valine, l-threonine, Gly-l-Phe, S-(-)-binaphthol) on the surface of magnetic nanoparticles. The prepared MIPs were then packed in the polydimethylsiloxane microchannel using a magnetic field as a novel stationary phase to perform the enantiomeric separation in a microchip electrophoresis system. The system provided satisfactory results by opening a new chamber for the chiral separation of molecules. Some other scientists [462] developed a calorimetric molecularly imprinted photonic polymer (MIPPs) sensor. The prepared sensor was used for the successful detection and chiral recognition of amino acids. The sensor was fabricated using the polystyrene photonic crystal template followed by thermal polymerization. The sensor was then used for the recognition of l-pyroglutamic acid (l-Pga) which was obvious from the gradual color change in MIPs sensor. Table 6 lists some of the chiral molecules recognition using MIPs-based sensors.

Some other attempts [463] were made for developing ultra-thin MIPs fill-based chiral electrochemical sensors for studying the molecular recognition mechanism. The system utilized methacrylate-basedR(+)-atenolol MIPs for the chiral sensing interface of the electrochemical sensor. The sensors were employed for the specific recognition of ferro/ferricyanide and FDM (redox probes) confirming the highly specific recognition and rebinding property of MIPs.

MIPs-based sensors appear to be a convenient choice for the selective and specific sensing and detection of explosives, chemical warfare agents, their precursors, and degradation products [478]. A group of scientists [479] discovered a novel nanosensor by MIPs integration with SERS. The sensor was used for the detection of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) explosive. The SERS-active surface was covered with micron-thick films of sol–gel xerogels as the sensing materials. The xerogel was imprinted (molecular imprinting) following the non-covalent approach for the selective detection of TNT. MIPs-SERS sensor exhibited a good sensing ability for TNT with LOD of 3 µM. Some other researchers [480] developed electrochemical sensors based on molecularly imprinted polydopamine films. The sensors were then used for the selective detection of nitro explosives in aqueous solutions such as TNT and RDX (Research Department Explosive, 1,3,5-trinitroperhydro-1,3,5-triazine) (Fig. 19 ). The operation was carried out by coating the gold electrodes with a thin film of molecularly imprinted polydopamine using electropolymerization. The results showed a good sensitivity of the electrochemical sensor toward the targeted analytes.

Other researchers [481] developed quartz crystal microbalance-based sensors for the detection of TNT. TNT-imprinted MIPs were synthesized and coated on the surface of the gold electrodes. The obtained results showed good sensitivity toward the explosives. Electrochemical sensors are also developed for the detection of explosive triacetone triperoxide (TATP) [482]. MIPs layer was developed on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode using electropolymerization. The sensor displayed a good response toward the targeted explosive with LOD of 26.9 μg/L and LOQ of 81.6 μg/L. Table 7 lists some MIPs-based sensors for explosives detection applications.

Another subject of interest is the sensing and quantification of the pest-killing agents used in crops. These pesticides, insecticides, herbicides, etc. have enormously been used to control pests and ultimately increase crop production. However, these pesticides sometimes accumulate within the crops making their way to the food products. Hence, it is important to detect the presence of these pesticides in crops and food products to avoid the health risks involved. For this purpose, MIPs-based sensors have been developed and utilized for selective and specific sensing of pathogens. An electrochemical impedimetric sensor was developed and used for the detection of methidathion organophosphorus insecticide [499]. The sensor was developed by incorporating MIPs with the sol–gel method onto the carbon electrode surface with a printed screen. The developed MIPs/sol–gel-based sensor showed a high response toward the methidathion organophosphorus detection with LOD of 5.14 µg/L. The highest selectivity of the developed sensor toward the specific methidathion organophosphorus insecticide was attributed to the development of the interactions between the functional groups of the targeted analyte with the sensor’s binding sites through hydrogen bonding.

Similarly, other laboratories [500] investigated the development of SPR sensors based on magnetic MIPs for the determination of chlorpyrifos pesticide. The development of the sensor was processed by magnetic MIPs design by sol–gel polymerization of dopamine on magnetic nanoparticles surface in the presence of a weak base. The biosensor showed a satisfactory response toward the organophosphorus chlorpyrifos CPF pesticide with a detection limit of 0.76 nM. The results justified further use of the biosensor for the selective detection of CPF and other pesticides [501]. Integration of molecularly imprinted nanofilms with SPR sensors for the detection of pesticides like cyanazine (SNZ), simazine (SMZ), and atrazine (ATZ) has also been investigated. Polymerization was carried out using UV and 1-vinylimidazole (VIM) as a co-monomer, N-methacryloyl-l-phenylalanine methyl ester (MAPA) as a functional monomer, and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) as a crosslinker. The sensor showed reasonable performance with LOD values of 0.095, 0.031, and 0.091 nM for SNZ, SMZ, and ATZ, respectively.

MIPs are used as biomarkers for various infectious diseases. The biomarker-based sensors derived from MIPs are suitable biological molecules for measuring a biological state. Such sensors proved to be excellent indicators of biological processes, pharmacological responses, or pathogenic processes. The deployment of MIPs-based biomarkers/sensors revolutionized the typical diagnostic tools for the detection of various infectious diseases. These biomarkers provided the chance to monitor various diseases on an advanced level and a better chance for their control. Mass spectrometry assays were analyzed using MIPs-based biomarkers for targeting the proteotypic peptide of ProGRP [530]. First, MIPs were synthesized following the surface-initiated reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer polymerization followed by its use for the ProGRP signature peptide for the serum samples. The product was used for the solid-phase extraction of the specific peptide proving to be an excellent choice for the analysis of the targeted phase extraction (Fig. 21 ).

The strategy of antibody-free biomarkers using MIPs onto a moveable valve paper-based electrochemical device (Bio-MIPs-ePADs) was analyzed [531]. MIPs were fabricated using electropolymerization enabling a continuous flow of the fluid during the clinical testing. The device was designed to perform the clinical analysis using carcinoembryonic antigen as the prototyping model target. The results showed that the cancer biomarker MIPs-based electrochemical device is a convenient source with LOD of 0.32 ng/mL. Similarly, another group [532] studied the specific detection of protein biomarkers using dual MIPs-based plasmonic immunosandwich assay. The specific imprinting nature of MIPs was employed with a detection scheme like chemiluminescence and SERS forming a promising tool for disease biomarkers. The dual MIPs-based plasmonic immunosandwich assay (duMIPs-PISA) was used for protein biomarkers in complex biological systems. During the device design, the plasmonic substrate was prepared using a C-terminal epitope-imprinted self-assembled gold nanoparticle monolayer-coated glass slide to specifically extract the targeted protein. Whereas the labeling of the captured protein was performed through N-terminal epitope-imprinted Raman responsive Ag@SiO2 nanoparticles as the nanotags. The duMIPs-PISA approach was promising for the detection of various diseases.

Site-specific post-imprinting modification of MIPs-nanocavities has been investigated using a modifiable functional monomer for prostate cancer biomarker recognition [533]. MIPs were designed using a non-covalent approach using atom transfer radical polymerization introducing a post-imprinting poly(ethylene glycol)-based capping agent. The preparation of MIPs employed a PSA-conjugated sensor chip using functional monomers that had carboxy and secondary amine groups for the interaction as well as post-imprinting, respectively. The conjugation of PSA on the sensor chip was carried out via diesters between the cis-diol groups of PSA glucans and the immobilized 3-fluorophenyl boronic acid. The developed agent showed satisfactory results for the specific recognition of glycoproteins to be used as a biomarker sensor. Table 9 summarizes the applications of MIPs as biomarkers.

The rise in the discharge of wastewater from industrial sources is prompting the development of solutions for wastewater remediation [555,556]. Among the most applied methods for the removal of contaminants is photocatalysis [557] and sorption [558].

The performance of MIPs as photocatalysts for the removal of ethyl p-hydroxybenzoate was analyzed [559]. MIPs were synthesized via the sol–gel method using ethyl p-hydroxybenzoate as a template and was then used to form Ag2S-MIPs-TiO2 nanocomposite for photocatalytic degradation with 92.22 % efficiency. The photocatalyst contains Ag2S, which provided the photo-generated carriers under UV source, while MIPs-TiO2 provided high selectivity toward the pollutant. The combined effect of Ag2S-MIPs-TiO2 nanocomposite increased the electron transport efficiency and decreased the recombination chance of the electrons and the holes. This operation facilitated the photocatalytic degradation of ethyl p-hydroxybenzoate by oxidation to water and carbon dioxide.

In another study, degradation and removal of the pharmaceutical contaminant ciprofloxacin were demonstrated using molecularly imprinted carbon nanosheets supported by TiO2 (MICT) via sorption and photocatalysis [560]. The obtained results showed high MICT efficiency toward ciprofloxacin removal with a selectivity coefficient of 3.2 using photocatalysis and 7.2 using sorption. The removal could be attributed to specific recognition, electroconductivity, carbon sorption, and photocatalytic degradation. The higher degradation efficiency of ciprofloxacin is attributed to active species like holes, ⋅O2 − and O⋅H. The cleavage of quinolone and piperazine rings resulted in the degradation of ciprofloxacin by the substitution of F atoms with hydroxyl functional groups (Fig. 22 ).

The sorption efficiency of MIPs in the removal of patulin, an organic compound classified as a polyketide, from apple juice was analyzed [561]. Fe3O4@SiO2@CS-GO@MIPs adsorbent was developed by using a surface imprinting strategy. MIPs were developed onto the surface of magnetic nanoparticles for the removal of the sorbent while chitosan and silicon dioxide enhanced the biocompatibility, dispersibility, and stability of the sorbent. The developed sorbent showed high efficiency of 90 % in the removal of patulin.

The sorption of methylxanthines and polycyclic aromatics using MIPs-based sorbents was investigated [562]. The sorbents were developed by the surface functionalization of caffeine-imprinted silica aerogels. Satisfactory results were obtained with a sorption capacity of 9.84 mg/g. The good efficiency of MIPs-based sorbent is associated with the π-π interaction of the sorbate with the sorbent due to the presence of aromatic rings. The sorption follows pseudo-second order kinetics and is influenced by the intra-particle diffusion within the pores. Table 10 summarizes the applications of MIPs for wastewater treatment.

In targeted drug delivery, the system targets the drug in body tissues, organs, or tumors, and then there is a regulation of the amount or rate of the release of the therapeutic agent. However, sometimes, the system of controlled delivery exhibits both capabilities at the same time. These good characteristics reduce the side effects, help prevent the drugs from affecting healthy organs, maintain the required therapeutic level, and prevent the high dose of the drug [596–599]. In the field of controlled drug delivery, MIPs are among the most promising materials [600–603]. The high affinity between the template and functional groups in the binding sites of MIPs network often results in a decrease in the speed of the template release. Therefore, it is needed to develop MIPs for the sustained release of the drugs. The drug rate of release and template utilization are regulated by the crosslinker and the amount of monomer in MIPs [604–607]. Furthermore, the application of stimuli-responsive MIPs is helpful in the targeted delivery system that can aid the template release in the response to a mentioned stimulus such as temperature, pH, ionic strength, light incident, or in the presence of some specific molecule on a given site or the tissue or cell surface [608–611].

Recently, an effective and simple method was designed and developed for the preparation of a targeted delivery system by employing MIPs together with magnetite nanoparticles [612]. To enhance the amount of magnetic molecular imprinted polymers (MMIPs) and to release the drug in a specific area, there is a need to localize MMIPs to a specific site by the response of the external magnetic field. Moreover, there are many advantages of magnetic MIPs over simple MIPs, as magnetic MIPs can easily be separated from the media [613–617].

Recently, several methods have been employed to control drug delivery [618] in which the drug delivery system (DDS) is the most fascinating one using MIPs as a drug carrier [619]. DDS process and its branches were studied in detail, and it was found that the delivery of the drug to the target is effective. Furthermore, under controlled release, it was important to administer the process for some time to achieve the desired therapeutic results, which were effectively metabolized and removed after administration [620]. In addition, a lower or higher dose of the drug may have an adverse effect or no effect after the delivery.

It is of high interest to investigate the stimuli-responsiveness of the device of the drug delivery for the safe and selective release of the loaded drug in the tumor cells. In a study, oxidizedcellulosenanoparticles were prepared by oxidation of cellulose with sodium periodate and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl radical.Doxorubicin(Dox) was used as an anticancer drug, which was loaded in the oxidizedcellulose using a pH-responsive interaction between the drug and oxidizedcellulose [618]. The process was observed at different pH values. It was found that there is a maximum release of Dox in tumor tissues at pH 5.0 (endosomal) and pH 6.8 (extracellular), while the release was low at pH 7.4 (physiological) of the normal tissues (Fig. 23 ).

In the case of tumor therapy, DDS is specifically designed for the timely targeting of cancer cells. Therefore, the drug carrier should be on the nanoscale and the pore size should be between 20 and 250 nm, with high structural and mechanical stability and negligible toxicity [621,622]. Regarding the requirements for drug delivery, MIPs meet all the physical and chemical criteria. The structural and mechanical stability of MIPs makes the oriented recognition and spatial configuration of the fixed sites. However, not all DDS based on MIPs reflected the complete mechanism of molecular imprinted synthetic antibodies (MISAs).

Liu and co-workers reported the development of glycan-targeting molecular imprinted synthetic antibodies (MISAs) coated gold nanorods (AuNRs) for possible photothermal therapy and cancer image targeting [623]. In the preparation of the template of molecular imprinted synthetic antibodies (MISAs), gold nanorods (AuNRs) were used as photothermal nanomaterials and sialic acid while increasing the frequency of glycan on cancer cell membranes. As a result, the prepared MISA-coated gold nanorods showed excellent cancer-targeting [623]. Table 11 contains some examples of the applications of MIPs in targeted drug delivery.

The separation of liquid and enantiomeric mixtures of chiral molecules is a critical need. The separation of such compounds ultimately leads to the purification of the given materials. Apart from chiral recognitions, several other molecules such as proteins can also be separated. MIPs play a key role in separation applications [656]. One of the most acknowledged strategies for separation purposes is chromatography. While performing the chromatographic separation, one of the primary features is the selectivity of the stationary phase toward the analyte [657]. MIPs, because of having exceptionally high selectivity toward enantiomeric analytes at the molecular levels, are the right choice as a stationary phase in HPLC [509]. The most applied separation techniques using MIPs is gas chromatography (GC) [658], liquid chromatography (LC) [659], gas chromatography, mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [660], and solid-phase extraction (SPE) [661].

This review gathered the basics of molecularly imprinted polymers and their applications in multiple fields. The outstanding sensitivity and selectivity of MIPs have benefited several fields. A massive innovation can be brought into the field of molecular imprinting by utilizing functional monomers and solvents other than the conventional ones to further strengthen their properties. Tissue engineering, cell culture, bioimaging, crystallization, and medical imaging fields are also making use of MIPs to their fullest. Recently, nanotechnology has been incorporated with molecular imprinting to obtain more advanced materials. The synthetic mechanism and research on the design of MIPs are still to be addressed. In addition, there is a need to control the solvent residue when organic solvents are used for the preparation of MIPs.

MIPs applications cover a wide range of scientific domains. While in the field of sensors, a few researchers are involved in the development of MIPs for future science. The solid-phase approach in the preparation of MIPs and their contact with printing permit, provide an innovative approach for soluble polymer-coated nanoparticles with the properties of molecular recognition, sub-nanomolar affinities, and applications in imaging, drug delivery, and diagnostics. Targeting some specific cells such as pathogenic bacteria or cancer cells using MIPs nanoparticles would help revolutionize the proposed clinical practice and develop some useful and personalized medicine. Furthermore, the incorporation of optical, thermo-responsive, magnetic, and photocatalytic materials will be more fruitful in the design and development of multifunctional hybrid molecularly imprinted materials. In addition, this will help the selective degradation of multi-targets in water samples, which will ensure the recognition, enrichment, and further degradation.

There are several take-home messages from this review such as the drugs analysis, explosives probing, and many more. However, there are a few shortcomings such as the lack of calculation studies. One more study that should be investigated using MIPs is forensics science, particularly explosives. Such direction will add interesting and deep knowledge in such a thrilling field.

There is an urgent need for efficient and green analysis of trace level pollutants in water samples, which is an effective approach to pollution supervision, food monitoring, and life-long health concerns. Therefore, convenient, fast, and efficient methods of wastewater sample analysis are desirable, which for sure need solvent-recognition MIPs with high selectivity, sensitivity, and accuracy to meet the needs for diverse applications.
